fragments.content: Vestibular disorders cause vertigo (illusion of motion), dizziness, imbalance. Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo (BPPV) is most common cause of vertigo—brief (<1 minute) episodes triggered by head position changes (rolling in bed, looking up), caused by otoconia dislodged into semicircular canals (posterior canal most common). Vestibular neuritis: acute prolonged vertigo (days-weeks) from viral inflammation of vestibular nerve, no hearing loss. Labyrinthitis: vestibular neuritis plus hearing loss (cochlear involvement). Ménière's disease: episodic vertigo (20 minutes to 12 hours), fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness; caused by endolymphatic hydrops (increased endolymph pressure). Vestibular migraine: vertigo episodes with migraine features or history. Bilateral vestibulopathy: oscillopsia (visual blurring with head movement), imbalance from bilateral vestibular loss (ototoxicity like gentamicin, autoimmune, degenerative). Central causes of vertigo: stroke (cerebellar, brainstem), multiple sclerosis, tumors (acoustic neuroma/vestibular schwannoma).

Vestibular disorder treatment depends on etiology. BPPV: canalith repositioning maneuvers—Epley maneuver for posterior canal (80-90% effective after 1-3 treatments), Semont maneuver alternative, barbecue roll for horizontal canal. No role for vestibular suppressants long-term. Vestibular neuritis/labyrinthitis: acute phase (first 24-72 hours) vestibular suppressants (meclizine, dimenhydrinate, benzodiazepines) and antiemetics; corticosteroids (prednisone taper) may improve recovery if started within 3 days. After acute phase: discontinue suppressants (impede compensation), start vestibular rehabilitation therapy (gaze stabilization, habituation, balance exercises). Ménière's disease: low-salt diet (<2g/day sodium), diuretics (HCTZ/triamterene), betahistine (H1 agonist/H3 antagonist, widely used outside US, limited evidence). Intratympanic steroids for refractory cases; intratympanic gentamicin for severe refractory vertigo (chemical labyrinthectomy—hearing loss risk). Endolymphatic sac surgery for refractory cases. Vestibular migraine: migraine preventives (topiramate, verapamil, venlafaxine, amitriptyline), trigger management. Bilateral vestibulopathy: vestibular rehabilitation (limited benefit if complete loss), fall prevention, avoid ototoxins. Acoustic neuroma: observation (small, asymptomatic), stereotactic radiosurgery, or microsurgical resection.

Vestibular diagnosis requires careful history and bedside examination. History: timing (episodic vs continuous), triggers (positional, spontaneous), duration (seconds = BPPV; minutes-hours = Ménière's, vestibular migraine; days = neuritis), associated symptoms (hearing loss, tinnitus, headache, focal neurologic signs). Dix-Hallpike test for BPPV: rapid head extension and rotation elicits torsional-upbeating nystagmus with latency and fatigability (posterior canal). Supine roll test for horizontal canal BPPV. HINTS exam for acute vestibular syndrome (differentiates stroke from neuritis): Head Impulse test (abnormal = peripheral), Nystagmus (direction-changing = central), Test of Skew (vertical deviation = central); HINTS more sensitive than MRI early in stroke. Audiometry: low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss in Ménière's. VNG (videonystagmography) assesses ocular motor and caloric function. vHIT (video head impulse test) quantifies vestibulo-ocular reflex. VEMP (vestibular evoked myogenic potentials) tests otolith function. Rotatory chair for bilateral vestibulopathy. MRI internal auditory canals with gadolinium for suspected acoustic neuroma, central causes. Orthostatic vitals for orthostatic hypotension mimicking dizziness.
